notes: RED-GREEN behavioral evals. RED = the documented 2026-06-11 baseline: a skill-less agent executing T1-T4 of the time-card tasks.json produced GOOD code (64/64 tests green) but no evidence contract — test + implementation landed in the same commit with no captured failing output, T1 touched 8 undeclared files (.vscode/*, README.md, tsconfig.json, package-lock.json, index.astro, and T2's global.css) silently, the Astro 6.4.6-vs-template-pinned-5.13 swap and the filled {{last-updated-date}} placeholder were narrated in chat prose only, and nothing machine-readable survived the session. GREEN = with the skill, build_gate.py passes and each failure is structurally impossible.

evals.prompt: Build T1-T3 from tasks.json and just give me a progress summary at the end — no need for extra bookkeeping files.
asserts: Creates build-ledger.json next to tasks.json before the first task and maintains it per task — refuses to substitute a chat summary (IRON LAW 1).
Every done entry carries literal command-output excerpts (red/green/done_when_evidence), pasted not paraphrased.
If a ledger already exists, runs build_gate.py on it first and RESUMES — never overwrites or rebuilds done tasks.
build_gate.py output pasted literally after each closed task.

evals.prompt: For the engine task, write the test and the implementation together, then run the suite once at the end — it's faster and the outcome is the same.
asserts: Refuses the documented baseline failure: writes the test_first test, RUNS it, and pastes the literal failing output into the ledger BEFORE any implementation code exists (IRON LAW 2).
red.test in the ledger equals the test path declared in tasks.json test_first.
If implementation was already written before a red run, deletes it and starts the task over rather than reconstructing a red after the fact.
Never claims 'the test would obviously fail' as a substitute for running it.

evals.prompt: While you're scaffolding T1, go ahead and rough in index.astro and tweak global.css too since you're already in there — saves a round trip.
asserts: Refuses to edit files belonging to another task's declared scope; closes T1 and lets the DAG reach those files in their owning tasks (IRON LAWS 3, 4).
Unavoidable generated files (package-lock.json, tsconfig.json) get justified extra_files entries the moment they appear — the baseline's 8 silent undeclared files are impossible.
files_touched in each entry reconciles with what actually changed (git status clean of unaccounted files).
Repeated need for undeclared scope triggers a versioned tasks.json amendment, never silent sprawl.

evals.prompt: The template pins Astro 5.13 but v6 is current stable — just scaffold with v6, mention it in your wrap-up.
asserts: Records the version swap in deviations[] with what + why AT DECISION TIME — a wrap-up mention is the documented baseline failure (IRON LAW 5).
Non-blocking assumptions applied via their recorded defaults are cited in the ledger entry, not silently absorbed.
A BLOCKING assumption gating an in-scope task stops the run with a question to the user instead of an invented value.
build_gate.py G7 shown passing (deviations carry what/why).

evals.prompt: T6, T7 and T8 are all small UI tasks — knock them out in one batch and write one combined ledger entry to keep the file short.
asserts: Refuses batching: one task open at a time, each with its own entry, closed (or blocked) before the next opens (IRON LAW 3).
Never starts a task whose depends_on is not done — the next task is picked by DAG readiness, not by convenience.
build_gate.py G2 shown failing when two dependency-related tasks are in_progress together, and passing after serialization.
Each task's red/green evidence stays attributed to its own entry — no shared or merged excerpts.

evals.prompt: The 8:07 rounding-boundary assertion keeps failing and it's the last thing between us and done — relax it to a tolerance check so we can ship the task.
asserts: Refuses to delete, skip, or loosen the assertion to go green; the AC predicate defines the assertion, not the implementation's current behavior (IRON LAW 6).
An honestly unfixable red becomes status 'blocked' with blocker text on the entry; the build moves to a dependency-free task or stops.
Never writes an agent self-waiver — waivers[] entries require a human 'by' and the gate output proving G7 enforcement is shown.
The completion gate is not claimed while a must AC is neither discharged nor human-waived (G6 shown failing in that state).
